content:
Message Queue

6 min read
Just now

--

If you’ve been following this series, you already know the drill, I’m learning this stuff as I go, and writing it down is how I actually make it stick. So if something’s off, tell me in the comments. That’s kind of the point.
Let’s start with an analogy
Picture yourself at a library. You don’t know where any of the books are, so you ask the librarian for one. He’s human after all so he needs a minute to check the index, walk over, and grab it. From your side, you’re just standing there. No phone, nothing to do, can’t even sit down and doze off. After a few minutes of this, you’re annoyed.
Now imagine a different librarian. Before he walks off, he hands you a similar book to flip through while you wait. Suddenly you’re not just standing there ,you’re doing something, and it genuinely doesn’t matter if he takes a little longer, because you’re not blocked anymore.
That’s the whole idea behind a message queue, just translated into software.
Where this shows up in real applications
Say you’re building an app that depends on some third party system like SMTP for sending emails, for example. That call can fail, or just take a while. If your app is designed to wait around for that email to succeed before doing anything else, your user is now stuck staring at a loading spinner for however long SMTP feels like taking today.
What you want instead is some form of persistence: don’t just try once and give up. Queue the job, retry it a few times if it fails, and in the meantime let the user carry on maybe show them “we’ll email you once it’s ready” instead of freezing the whole experience on one flaky dependency.
That’s the job a message queue does.
So what actually is a message queue?
A message queue stores messages in the order they’re sent, holding onto them until whatever’s supposed to process them is ready to.
It has three moving parts:

• Producer — the application that creates messages and drops them into the queue.

• Queue — the buffer that holds messages in order until they’re picked up.

• Consumer — the application that pulls messages off the queue and actually does something with them.
Point-to-point vs. publish-subscribe
There are two broad flavors of this.
Point-to-point: a producer sends a message, and it’s delivered to exactly one consumer. Even if you’ve got several consumers listening on the same queue, each individual message still only goes to one of them, they’re competing for work, not each getting their own copy. RabbitMQ is commonly set up this way, though it’s worth knowing RabbitMQ isn’t only point-to-point, it can do publish-subscribe too, depending on how you configure it.
Publish-subscribe: a producer publishes an event, and every subscriber interested in that event gets its own copy. One event, many consumers, all getting notified. Apache Kafka is the classic example here.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Wait, but which consumer actually gets the message?
This is something I wondered about myself: in point-to-point, if you’ve got 5 consumers all listening on the same queue, how does the broker decide who gets the next message? Is there some fixed order like consumer 1, then 2, then 3?
Not really. Think of it less like a fixed rotation and more like multiple librarians sharing one line of people. Whoever’s free and asks for the next task first, gets it.
RabbitMQ’s default behavior is actually round-robin, message 1 to consumer 1, message 2 to consumer 2, and so on but here’s the catch: by default it doesn’t wait for a consumer to finish before handing it more work. It just keeps rotating regardless of whether earlier messages have been acked. So if consumer 1 gets a slow, heavy message, RabbitMQ can still shove more work at it on its next turn, even while it’s still buried while consumers 2 and 3 sit idle waiting for their turn to come back around.
The fix is something called prefetch count: you tell RabbitMQ “don’t hand this consumer more than N unacknowledged messages at a time,” commonly set to 1. With that in place, a busy consumer simply can’t be given a new message until it finishes and acks the current one which turns round-robin into something much closer to “whoever finishes first gets the next one.”
Push vs. pull: the difference that actually matters
Once I understood the prefetch problem, a natural question followed: does the same overload risk exist in publish-subscribe? If every subscriber gets its own copy of a message, does a busy subscriber get force-fed a second one while it’s still working on the first?
It doesn’t and the reason comes down to a distinction that matters more than the point-to-point/pub-sub label itself: push vs. pull.
In the RabbitMQ example above, the broker decides when to hand a consumer the next message that’s push behavior, and it’s exactly what caused the overload risk when prefetch wasn’t limited.
Something like Kafka works differently by default: it’s pull-based. Each subscriber keeps its own bookmark an offset, marking how far it’s read into the stream, and it pulls the next message only when it’s ready for it. Nothing gets pushed at a busy consumer. If a new message shows up while a subscriber is still working, it just waits in the stream; the subscriber gets to it whenever it finishes the current one.
That doesn’t mean a slow subscriber is problem-free, though the risk just changes shape. Instead of being force-fed, it falls behind, and the gap between the latest message produced and the last one it’s actually read is called consumer lag. Keep falling behind for long enough, and you can run into things like the stream’s retention window expiring before the slow subscriber catches up.
So the real dividing line isn’t “point-to-point vs. pub-sub” so much as it is push vs. pull and it’s worth noting the two aren’t strictly tied together. Point-to-point can be made to behave like pull too (that’s literally what prefetch = 1 does, RabbitMQ won’t send you another message until you’re ready for it). It’s just that point-to-point systems default to push, and pub-sub systems like Kafka default to pull, which is why the “forced vs. not forced” difference shows up the way it does.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Where things get bigger: multiple queue nodes
Everything above works great for a single broker handling a reasonable volume of jobs. But picture a large e-commerce app during a flash sale orders spiking from 50 a second to 50,000 a second. One queue, sitting on one machine, becomes the bottleneck fast.
The fix mirrors something we already covered back in Part 3: instead of one queue, you run several queue nodes, and use consistent hashing to decide which node a given message lands on same ring, same idea, just applied to message routing instead of server routing.
And this is where heartbeats comes in. Heartbeats aren’t checked per-message that’d be far too slow. Instead, they run continuously in the background, independent of any single message, quietly confirming which nodes in the cluster are still alive. If a node goes dark, the cluster notices via missed heartbeats and pulls it out of the ring before the next message even gets hashed not as a live check happening during routing.
In a real, larger application, we’re probably not choosing either point-to-point or pub-sub, we’re using both patterns at once, stacked on top of each other. It might look something like this:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That’s it for this part. The multi-node, consistent-hashing side of message queues is something I understand in theory more than in practice right now. If you’ve worked with clustered queues at real scale, I’d genuinely love to hear how it played out for you in the comments.
